After centuries of relative ethnic diversity, the population of modern [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") has become nearly completely ethnically homogeneous [Polish](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people") as a result of altered borders and the Nazi German and Soviet or [Polish Communist](/wiki/History_of_Poland_(1945%E2%80%931989) "History of Poland (1945–1989)") population transfers, expulsions and deportations ([from or to Poland](/wiki/Polish_population_transfers_(1944%E2%80%931946) "Polish population transfers (1944–1946)")) during and after [World War II](/wiki/History_of_Poland_(1939%E2%80%931945) "History of Poland (1939–1945)"). [Ethnic minorities](/wiki/Ethnic_minorities "Ethnic minorities") remain in Poland, however, including some newly arrived or increased in number. Ethnic groups include Germans, Ukrainians and [Belarusians](/wiki/Belarusian_minority_in_Poland "Belarusian minority in Poland").

## Historic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ethnic_minorities_in_Poland&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Historic")]

Main article: [Demographic history of Poland](/wiki/Demographic_history_of_Poland "Demographic history of Poland")

### Kingdom of Poland and the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ethnic_minorities_in_Poland&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Kingdom of Poland and the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth")]

Although the concept of an ethnic minority is mostly used about a modern period, Poland has historically been a multi-ethnic country. The early influx of [Czechs](/wiki/Czechs "Czechs"), [Hungarians](/wiki/Hungarians "Hungarians"), [Slovaks](/wiki/Slovaks "Slovaks"), and Germans was particularly notable, and they formed significant minorities (or majorities) in urban centers. [Walloons](/wiki/Walloons_in_Poland "Walloons in Poland") migrated to Poland probably since the 12th century, however, the first written mention of Walloon immigrants in [Wrocław](/wiki/Wroc%C5%82aw "Wrocław") comes from c. 1270.[[1]](#cite_note-1) [Armenians](/wiki/Armenians_in_Poland "Armenians in Poland") and [Scots](/wiki/Scots_in_Poland "Scots in Poland"), who formed notable communities, lived in Poland since the 14th century. After the late-14th-century [Polish–Lithuanian union](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_union "Polish–Lithuanian union") and the [Union of Lublin](/wiki/Union_of_Lublin "Union of Lublin"), which established the [Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth") in 1569, [Lithuanians](/wiki/Lithuanians "Lithuanians") and [Ruthenians](/wiki/Ruthenians "Ruthenians") became part of the population.

A 1493 estimate listed the combined population of Poland and Lithuania at 7.5 million, broken down by ethnicity:

* 3.25 million [Poles](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people")
* 3.75 million [Ruthenians](/wiki/Ruthenians "Ruthenians") (present-day [Ukrainians](/wiki/Ukrainians "Ukrainians"), [Belarusians](/wiki/Belarusians "Belarusians"), and [Rusyns](/wiki/Rusyns "Rusyns"))
* 0.5 million [Lithuanians](/wiki/Lithuanians "Lithuanians")[[2]](#cite_note-Pogonowski1-2)

In 1618, after the [Truce of Deulino](/wiki/Truce_of_Deulino "Truce of Deulino"), the Commonwealth's territory increased and its population reached 12 million. Its inhabitants could be roughly divided into:

* Poles – 4.5 million
* [Ukrainians](/wiki/Ukrainians_in_Poland "Ukrainians in Poland") – 3.5 million
* Belarusians – 1.5 million
* [Lithuanians](/wiki/Lithuanian_minority_in_Poland "Lithuanian minority in Poland") – 0.75 million
* [Prussians](/wiki/Old_Prussians "Old Prussians") – 0.75 million
* [Jews](/wiki/Jews_in_Poland "Jews in Poland") – 0.5 million
* [Livonians](/wiki/Latvians "Latvians") – 0.5 million

At that time, the *[szlachta](/wiki/Szlachta "Szlachta")* (nobility) were 10 percent of the population and the [burghers](/wiki/Bourgeoisie "Bourgeoisie") 15 percent.[[3]](#cite_note-Pogonowski-3)

With the population and territorial losses of the mid- and late 17th century, the 1717 population of the Commonwealth had declined to nine million in the following ethnic groups:

* 4.5 million Poles
* 1.5 million Ukrainians
* 1.2 million Belarusians
* 0.8 million Lithuanians
* 0.5 million Jews
* 0.5 million others[[3]](#cite_note-Pogonowski-3)

### Second Polish Republic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ethnic_minorities_in_Poland&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Second Polish Republic")]

Languages of instruction in interwar Polish schools and ethnic "mother tongues", 1937–38

According to the [1921 Polish census](/wiki/1921_Polish_census "1921 Polish census"), 30.8 percent of the population were ethnic minorities.[[4]](#cite_note-MJ-4) This increased due to the Polish victory in the [Polish-Soviet War](/wiki/Polish-Soviet_War "Polish-Soviet War") and the large territorial gains in the east as a consequence. According to the [1931 Polish census](/wiki/1931_Polish_census "1931 Polish census") (as cited by [Norman Davies](/wiki/Norman_Davies "Norman Davies")),[[5]](#cite_note-Davies-5) 68.9 percent of the population was Polish, 13.9 percent were Ukrainians, about 10 percent Jewish, 3.1 percent Belarusians, 2.3 percent Germans and 2.8 percent other groups (including [Lithuanians](/wiki/Lithuanians "Lithuanians"), [Czechs](/wiki/Czechs "Czechs") and [Armenians](/wiki/Armenians "Armenians")). There were also smaller communities of [Russians](/wiki/Russians "Russians") and [Romani people](/wiki/Romani_people "Romani people"). The minority situation was complex and fluid during the period.

Poland was also a nation of many religions. In 1921, 16,057,229 Poles (about 62.5 percent) were [Roman Catholics](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), 3,031,057 (about 11.8 percent) were [Eastern Rite Catholics](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic_Churches "Eastern Catholic Churches") (primarily [Ukrainian Greek](/wiki/Ukrainian_Greek_Catholic_Church "Ukrainian Greek Catholic Church") and [Armenian Rite Catholics](/wiki/Armenian_Catholic_Church "Armenian Catholic Church")), 2,815,817 (about 10.95 percent) were [Greek Orthodox](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox_Church "Russian Orthodox Church"), 2,771,949 (about 10.8 percent) were [Jewish](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism"), and 940,232 (about 3.7 percent) were Protestants (mostly [Lutherans](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism")).[[6]](#cite_note-PSL-6) Poland had the world's second-largest Jewish population by 1931: one-fifth, about 3,136,000.[[4]](#cite_note-MJ-4)

## People's Republic and Third Republic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ethnic_minorities_in_Poland&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: People's Republic and Third Republic")]

### Polish People's Republic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ethnic_minorities_in_Poland&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Polish People's Republic")]

Before [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), one-third of Poland's population belonged to ethnic [minority groups](/wiki/Minority_group "Minority group"). Poland's minorities were mostly gone after the war, however, due to the 1945 [revision of borders](/wiki/Territorial_changes_of_Poland_immediately_after_World_War_II "Territorial changes of Poland immediately after World War II") and [the Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust"). Under the [National Repatriation Office](/wiki/National_Repatriation_Office "National Repatriation Office") (*Państwowy Urząd Repatriacyjny*), millions of Poles were [forced to leave their homes](/wiki/Polish_population_transfers_(1944%E2%80%931946) "Polish population transfers (1944–1946)") in the eastern [Kresy](/wiki/Kresy "Kresy") region and settle in [territories regained from Germany](/wiki/Recovered_Territories "Recovered Territories") in the west. About five million remaining Germans (about eight million had already fled or been expelled, and about one million had been killed between 1944 and 1946) were similarly [expelled](/wiki/Flight_and_expulsion_of_Germans_(1944%E2%80%931950) "Flight and expulsion of Germans (1944–1950)") from those territories to [Allied-occupied Germany](/wiki/Allied-occupied_Germany "Allied-occupied Germany") in accordance with the [Potsdam Agreement](/wiki/Potsdam_Agreement "Potsdam Agreement"). Ukrainian and Belarusian minorities found themselves now mostly within the borders of the Soviet Union; those who opposed this new policy (like the [Ukrainian Insurgent Army](/wiki/Ukrainian_Insurgent_Army "Ukrainian Insurgent Army") in the [Bieszczady Mountains](/wiki/Bieszczady_Mountains "Bieszczady Mountains") region) were suppressed by the end of 1947 in [Operation Vistula](/wiki/Operation_Vistula "Operation Vistula").

The Jewish population of Poland, the largest Jewish community in pre-war Europe at about 3.3 million people, was almost completely destroyed by 1945. Approximately three million Jews died of starvation in [ghettos](/wiki/Jewish_ghettos_established_by_Nazi_Germany "Jewish ghettos established by Nazi Germany") and [labor camps](/wiki/Labor_camp "Labor camp"), or were slaughtered in Nazi [extermination camps](/wiki/Extermination_camp "Extermination camp") or by [Einsatzgruppen](/wiki/Einsatzgruppen "Einsatzgruppen") death squads. Between 40,000 and 100,000 Polish Jews survived the Holocaust in Poland, another 50,000 to 170,000 were repatriated from the Soviet Union, and 20,000 to 40,000 came from Germany and other countries. There were 180,000 to 240,000 Jews in Poland at the country's postwar peak, settled mainly in [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw"), [Łódź](/wiki/%C5%81%C3%B3d%C5%BA "Łódź"), [Kraków](/wiki/Krak%C3%B3w "Kraków") and [Wrocław](/wiki/Wroc%C5%82aw "Wrocław").[[7]](#cite_note-yivo-7)

### Third Polish Republic rights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ethnic_minorities_in_Poland&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Third Polish Republic rights")]

The [rights of ethnic minorities](/wiki/Minority_rights "Minority rights") in Poland are guaranteed in article 35 of the 1997 [Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Poland "Constitution of Poland"):

1. The Republic of Poland shall ensure Polish citizens belonging to national or ethnic minorities the freedom to maintain and develop their own language, to maintain customs and traditions, and to develop their own culture.
2. National and ethnic minorities shall have the right to establish educational and cultural institutions, institutions designed to protect religious identity, as well as to participate in the resolution of matters connected with their cultural identity.

The Act on Ethnic and National Minorities and on the Regional Language of 6 January 2005 ([Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language"): *Ustawa o mniejszościach narodowych i etnicznych oraz o języku regionalnym*)[[8]](#cite_note-act-8) stipulates that to be recognized as an ethnic or national minority, a group must reside in Poland for at least 100 years; this excludes minorities recognized by the Communist regime, such as the [Greeks](/wiki/Greeks_in_Poland "Greeks in Poland").[[9]](#cite_note-krakow-9) There are three categories of recognized minorities in Poland: nine national minorities (Belarusians, Czechs, Lithuanians, Germans, Armenians, Russians, Slovaks, Ukrainians and Jews), four ethnic minorities (Karaites, Lemkos, Roma and Tatars), and the regional [Kashubian](/wiki/Kashubians "Kashubians") linguistic minority.[[8]](#cite_note-act-8)[[10]](#cite_note-MSWIA-10)

Poland ratified the [European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages](/wiki/European_Charter_for_Regional_or_Minority_Languages "European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages") on 12 February 2009:[[11]](#cite_note-coe-11)

1. Minority languages: [Belarusian](/wiki/Belarusian_language "Belarusian language"), [Czech](/wiki/Czech_language "Czech language"), [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew "Hebrew"), [Yiddish](/wiki/Yiddish "Yiddish"), [Karaim](/wiki/Karaim_language "Karaim language"), [Kashubian](/wiki/Kashubian_language "Kashubian language"), [Lithuanian](/wiki/Lithuanian_language "Lithuanian language"), the [Lemko dialects](/wiki/Rusyn_language "Rusyn language"), [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"), [Armenian](/wiki/Armenian_language "Armenian language"), [Romani](/wiki/Romani_language "Romani language"), [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"), [Slovak](/wiki/Slovak_language "Slovak language"), [Tatar](/wiki/Tatar_language "Tatar language") and [Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language")
2. Regional language: Kashubian
3. National-minorities languages: Belarusian, Czech, Hebrew, Yiddish, Lithuanian, German, Armenian, Russian, Slovak and Ukrainian
4. Ethnic-minority languages: Karaim, Lemko, Romani and Tatar
5. Non-territorial languages: Hebrew, Yiddish, Karaim, Armenian and Romani

Minorities have a number of rights, including street signs and education in their native language, cultural development and non-assimilation. In municipalities (*[gminy](/wiki/Gmina "Gmina")*) where they constitute more than 20 percent of the population, they have the right to official communications in their native language. Such municipalities must be included on the official register of municipalities where an additional language is used, and incentives exist for officials of these municipalities to learn the regional language.[[8]](#cite_note-act-8)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ethnic_minorities_in_Poland&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Demographics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Mniejszo%C5%9Bci_w_Polsce_2021.png/250px-Mniejszo%C5%9Bci_w_Polsce_2021.png)](/wiki/File:Mniejszo%C5%9Bci_w_Polsce_2021.png)

Map of Poland showing areas that are at least 5% inhabited by ethnic minorities

In the [Polish census of 2002](/wiki/Polish_census_of_2002 "Polish census of 2002"), 96.7 percent claimed [Polish nationality](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people") and 97.8 percent said that they speak [Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language") at home.[[12]](#cite_note-census2002-12) In the 2011 census, 1.44 percent of Poland's 39 million inhabitants said that they had an ancestry other than Polish. That figure included 418,000 who identified as Silesian (362,000 as a single ethnicity and 391,000 as a second ethnicity) and 17,000 Kashubians (16,000 as a single ethnicity). Recognized minorities were 0.3 percent of the population: 49,000 Germans (26,000 a single ethnicity), 36,000 Ukrainians (26,000 single-ethnicity), 7,000 Lemkos (5,000 single-ethnicity), 37,000 Belarusians (31,000 single-ethnicity), 12,000 Roma people (9,000 single-ethnicity), and 8,000 Russians (5,000 single-ethnicity); 0.2 percent of the population were foreign citizens.[[13]](#cite_note-polskieradio-13)[[14]](#cite_note-stat-14)

In Poland, the following minorities are recognized as national minorities: Belarusian, Czech, Lithuanian, German, Armenian, Russian, Slovak, Ukrainian and Jewish, and as ethnic minorities: Karaim, Lemko, Roma and Tatar.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Dutch

French
:   Further information: [French people in Poland](/wiki/French_people_in_Poland "French people in Poland")

    Historically, there were three major surges of French migration to Poland, consisting of persecuted [Huguenots](/wiki/Huguenots "Huguenots") following the [St. Bartholomew's Day massacre](/wiki/St._Bartholomew%27s_Day_massacre "St. Bartholomew's Day massacre") of 1572, then in the 17th century when French noblewomen [Marie Louise Gonzaga](/wiki/Marie_Louise_Gonzaga "Marie Louise Gonzaga") and [Marie Casimire Louise de la Grange d'Arquien](/wiki/Marie_Casimire_Louise_de_la_Grange_d%27Arquien "Marie Casimire Louise de la Grange d'Arquien") were Queens consorts of Poland, and the flight of French monarchists, merchants and craftsmen from the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution") in the late 18th century.[[23]](#cite_note-23) There were 1,633 [French](/wiki/French_people "French people") in the 2002 census, including 1,068 Polish citizens.

Germans
:   Further information: [German minority in Poland](/wiki/German_minority_in_Poland "German minority in Poland")

    Ethnic [Germans](/wiki/Germans "Germans") remain in [Silesia](/wiki/Silesia "Silesia"), [Pomerania](/wiki/Pomerania "Pomerania"), [Warmia-Masuria](/wiki/Warmian-Masurian_Voivodeship "Warmian-Masurian Voivodeship") and [Lubusz Land](/wiki/Lubusz_Land "Lubusz Land"). According to the 2002 census, 147,094 Germans live primarily in the region of [Opole](/wiki/Opole "Opole"), [Katowice](/wiki/Katowice "Katowice") and [Częstochowa](/wiki/Cz%C4%99stochowa "Częstochowa") (southern Poland).[[10]](#cite_note-MSWIA-10) Germans first came to Silesia during the [Late Middle Ages](/wiki/Late_Middle_Ages "Late Middle Ages"),[[24]](#cite_note-24) and largely either fled from the advancing [Eastern Front](/wiki/Eastern_Front_(World_War_II) "Eastern Front (World War II)") during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") or were expelled in accordance with the [Potsdam Agreement](/wiki/Potsdam_Agreement "Potsdam Agreement") after the war.

Gorals
:   Polish [Gorals](/wiki/Gorals "Gorals") are an ethnic group in southern Poland who speak a Polish dialect which has been heavily influenced by Slovak. Some urban Poles find this dialect difficult to understand.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Polish Gorals live primarily in the region of [Podhale](/wiki/Podhale "Podhale") and are separated from Slovak Gorals by the [High Tatra](/wiki/High_Tatra "High Tatra") mountains, which (with [Zakopane Style](/wiki/Zakopane_Style "Zakopane Style") architecture) are an important part of Goral identity and part of the reason why [Zakopane](/wiki/Zakopane "Zakopane") is a popular tourist destination and winter resort town.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] A Nazi attempt to Germanize the *[Goralenvolk](/wiki/Goralenvolk "Goralenvolk")* during its World War II occupation of Poland was largely unsuccessful.

Greeks
:   Further information: [Greeks in Poland](/wiki/Greeks_in_Poland "Greeks in Poland")

    [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/2012_Zgorzelec_07.jpg/250px-2012_Zgorzelec_07.jpg)](/wiki/File:2012_Zgorzelec_07.jpg)

    Four to five thousand [Greeks](/wiki/Greeks "Greeks") live in central and southeast Poland, most of whom came in 1949, after the [Greek Civil War](/wiki/Greek_Civil_War "Greek Civil War"). It is estimated that after this conflict, some 14,000 Greeks came to Poland, settling mainly in the town of [Zgorzelec](/wiki/Zgorzelec "Zgorzelec") in [Lower Silesia](/wiki/Lower_Silesia "Lower Silesia"). Among famous Poles of Greek origin are musicians [Eleni Tzoka](/wiki/Eleni_Tzoka "Eleni Tzoka") and [Apostolis Anthimos](/wiki/Apostolis_Anthimos "Apostolis Anthimos").

Jews
:   Further information: [History of the Jews in Poland](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Poland "History of the Jews in Poland")

    Poland had the world's largest population of [Jews](/wiki/Jew "Jew") for many centuries, and Jews were Poland's first minority group. However, the community did not survive [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). Before the war, there were 3,474,000 Jews in Poland. 90% of Polish Jews were killed during the [Holocaust](/wiki/Holocaust "Holocaust") following the [German invasion of Poland](/wiki/German_invasion_of_Poland "German invasion of Poland"). Those who escaped mostly went to the United States, Israel, Great Britain or Latin America. Many survivors willingly emigrated or were expelled by the Communists after the war. In the 2002 census, there were 1,055 Jewish people in Poland.[[10]](#cite_note-MSWIA-10) In the 2011 census, that number increased to 7,353.[[26]](#cite_note-26) They live primarily in large cities such as [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw"), [Wrocław](/wiki/Wroc%C5%82aw "Wrocław"), [Kraków](/wiki/Krak%C3%B3w "Kraków") and [Lublin](/wiki/Lublin "Lublin").

Kashubians
:   In the [Polish census of 2002](/wiki/Polish_census_of_2002 "Polish census of 2002"), 5,100 people declared [Kashubian](/wiki/Kashubians "Kashubians") ethnicity; 52,665 declared Kashubian as their native language. In ten municipalities, more than 20 percent of the population spoke Kashubian: [Przodkowo](/wiki/Gmina_Przodkowo "Gmina Przodkowo") (49 percent), [Sulęczyno](/wiki/Gmina_Sul%C4%99czyno "Gmina Sulęczyno") (48.6 percent), [Stężyca](/wiki/Gmina_St%C4%99%C5%BCyca,_Pomeranian_Voivodeship "Gmina Stężyca, Pomeranian Voivodeship") (43.2 percent), [Sierakowice](/wiki/Gmina_Sierakowice "Gmina Sierakowice") (39.9 percent), [Linia](/wiki/Gmina_Linia "Gmina Linia") (35.5 percent), [Chmielno](/wiki/Gmina_Chmielno "Gmina Chmielno") (34.8 percent), [Puck](/wiki/Puck,_Poland "Puck, Poland") (30.9 percent), [Somonino](/wiki/Gmina_Somonino "Gmina Somonino") (30.8 percent), [Szemud](/wiki/Gmina_Szemud "Gmina Szemud") (26.3 percent) and [Parchowo](/wiki/Gmina_Parchowo "Gmina Parchowo") (22.6 percent).[[12]](#cite_note-census2002-12) In the 2011 census, the number of people citing Kashubian as their first single ethnicity increased to 17,000; 229,000 declared Kashubian as their first or second ethnicity.[[16]](#cite_note-stat1-16)

Kursenieki
:   [![Map of the Baltic coast where the Kursenieki lived in 1649](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Curonians_kursenieki_in_1649.png/250px-Curonians_kursenieki_in_1649.png)](/wiki/File:Curonians_kursenieki_in_1649.png)

    The Kursenieki-populated area in 1649

    The [Kursenieki](/wiki/Kursenieki "Kursenieki"), also known as Kuronowie Pruscy and Kurończycy in Polish and Kuršininkai in Lithuanian, are a nearly-extinct [Baltic](/wiki/Balts "Balts") ethnic group living along the [Curonian Spit](/wiki/Curonian_Spit "Curonian Spit"). They were assimilated by the Germans except along the Curonian Spit, where some still live.

Lemkos and Rusyns
:   In the 2002 census, 5,850 Polish citizens declared themselves [Lemkos](/wiki/Lemkos "Lemkos") and 62 identified as [Rusyns](/wiki/Rusyns "Rusyns"). In the 2011 census, 10,000 people declared Lemkos as their first or second ethnicity.

Lithuanians
:   Further information: [Lithuanian minority in Poland](/wiki/Lithuanian_minority_in_Poland "Lithuanian minority in Poland")

    There were 5,846 Lithuanians in Poland (5,639 Polish citizens), according to the 2002 census. They live in close concentrations in [Suwałki](/wiki/Suwa%C5%82ki "Suwałki") in north-eastern Poland and in [Puńsk Municipality](/wiki/Gmina_Pu%C5%84sk "Gmina Puńsk"), where they were 74.4 percent of the population.

Macedonians
:   Further information: [Macedonians in Poland](/wiki/Macedonians_in_Poland "Macedonians in Poland")

    There were 286 [Macedonians](/wiki/Macedonians_(ethnic_group) "Macedonians (ethnic group)") in Poland in the 2002 census, including 187 Polish citizens. Five thousand Macedonian speakers were mentioned in 1970.[[27]](#cite_note-wissenschaftliche-27)

Masurians
:   There were 46 self-declared [Masurians](/wiki/Masurians "Masurians") in the 2002 census, all Polish citizens.

Romani
:   There were 12,731 [Romani people in Poland](/wiki/Romani_people_in_Poland "Romani people in Poland"), according to the 2002 census.[[10]](#cite_note-MSWIA-10) The Polish Roma population suffered heavily from their attempted extermination by Germany during World War II. They are dispersed and live around the country, although they are more numerous in the south.

Russians
:   Further information: [Russian minority in Poland](/wiki/Russian_minority_in_Poland "Russian minority in Poland")

    [Russians](/wiki/Russians "Russians") are scattered around Poland, but live mainly in the east. There were 3,244 Russians in Poland in the 2002 census.[[10]](#cite_note-MSWIA-10) This includes [Old Believers](/wiki/Old_Believers "Old Believers"), numbering two to three thousand in north-eastern Poland.

Scots
:   Further information: [Scots in Poland](/wiki/Scots_in_Poland "Scots in Poland")

    [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Szkocja%2C_powiat_suwalski%2C_2018-08-06_%2803%29.jpg/250px-Szkocja%2C_powiat_suwalski%2C_2018-08-06_%2803%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Szkocja,_powiat_suwalski,_2018-08-06_(03).jpg)

    Modern view of [Szkocja](/wiki/Szkocja,_Podlaskie_Voivodeship "Szkocja, Podlaskie Voivodeship") (Polish for *Scotland*), a village founded for Scottish settlers

    In 1380, the first Scots settled in [Gdańsk](/wiki/Gda%C5%84sk "Gdańsk"), founding what would eventually become a significant Scottish diaspora in Poland.[[28]](#cite_note-28) [Scottish people](/wiki/Scottish_people "Scottish people") migrated to Poland in large numbers in the mid-16th century. Mostly from the [Highlands](/wiki/Highlands_of_Scotland "Highlands of Scotland") and mainly [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic") and [Episcopalian](/wiki/Scottish_Episcopal_Church "Scottish Episcopal Church"), they fled religious persecution and harsh economic conditions. There was extensive trade between Scotland's eastern ports, such as [Dundee](/wiki/Dundee "Dundee"), [Leith](/wiki/Leith "Leith") and [Aberdeen](/wiki/Aberdeen "Aberdeen"), and towns such as Gdańsk and Königsberg (modern [Kaliningrad](/wiki/Kaliningrad "Kaliningrad")).[[29]](#cite_note-29) [William Lithgow](/wiki/William_Lithgow_(traveller_and_author) "William Lithgow (traveller and author)"), who visited Poland in 1616, reported an estimated 30,000 Scottish families living in the country which he described as "... a mother and nurse for the youth and younglings of Scotland ... in cloathing, feeding, and inrichening them".[[30]](#cite_note-30) Many came from Dundee and Aberdeen, and could be found in towns on the banks of the [Vistula](/wiki/Vistula "Vistula") as far south as Kraków. It is believed that many Poles have unacknowledged Scottish ancestry.[[31]](#cite_note-inyourpocket-31)[[32]](#cite_note-Academic_studies_Poland's_Scots-32) There were 26 self-declared Scots in the 2011 census, including 13 Polish citizens.

Silesians
:   Further information: [Silesians](/wiki/Silesians "Silesians")

    In the 2002 census, 173,153 people declared Silesian ethnicity and about 60,000 listed [Silesian](/wiki/Silesian_language "Silesian language") as their [native language](/wiki/Native_language "Native language"). In the 2011 census, Silesian ethnicity was declared by 809,000 responders out of five million in the region (including 362,000 who declared it as their only ethnicity, 418,000 who declared it as their first ethnicity, and 415,000 who declared it with a Polish ethnicity.[[33]](#cite_note-wyborcza-33)

Slovaks
:   [Slovaks](/wiki/Slovaks "Slovaks") live in southern Poland, and there were 1,710 in the 2002 census.[[10]](#cite_note-MSWIA-10) Polish Slovaks inhabit two small frontier regions in [Spisz](/wiki/Spisz "Spisz") and [Orawa](/wiki/Orava_(region) "Orava (region)") (near the Polish-Slovak border). Larger groups of Slovaks live in [Kraków](/wiki/Krak%C3%B3w "Kraków") and [Silesia](/wiki/Silesia "Silesia").

Swedes
:   Swedes live in [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw"), [Szczecin](/wiki/Szczecin "Szczecin"), [Kamień Pomorski](/wiki/Kamie%C5%84_Pomorski "Kamień Pomorski"), [Gdańsk](/wiki/Gda%C5%84sk "Gdańsk"), and [Wrocław](/wiki/Wroc%C5%82aw "Wrocław").

Tatars
:   Further information: [Lipka Tatars](/wiki/Lipka_Tatars "Lipka Tatars")

    [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/SM_Bohoniki_Meczet_2020_%285%29.jpg/250px-SM_Bohoniki_Meczet_2020_%285%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:SM_Bohoniki_Meczet_2020_(5).jpg)

    [Bohoniki Mosque](/wiki/Bohoniki_Mosque "Bohoniki Mosque")

    Small populations of Polish [Lipka Tatars](/wiki/Lipka_Tatars "Lipka Tatars") practice [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"). Some Polish villages, mainly in northeastern Poland's [Podlaskie Voivodeship](/wiki/Podlaskie_Voivodeship "Podlaskie Voivodeship"), have [mosques](/wiki/Mosque "Mosque"). Tatars arrived as mercenary soldiers during the late 14th century. The 2002 census listed 447 people declaring this ethnicity.[[10]](#cite_note-MSWIA-10)

Ukrainians
:   Further information: [Ukrainians in Poland](/wiki/Ukrainians_in_Poland "Ukrainians in Poland")

    See also: [Ukrainian refugee crisis (2022–present)](/wiki/Ukrainian_refugee_crisis_(2022%E2%80%93present) "Ukrainian refugee crisis (2022–present)")

    Before the [Russian-Ukrainian War](/wiki/Russian-Ukrainian_War "Russian-Ukrainian War"), [Ukrainians](/wiki/Ukrainians "Ukrainians") in Poland were scattered throughout eastern and northern districts. In the Polish census of 2002, 27,172 people declared that they belonged to this group.[[10]](#cite_note-MSWIA-10)

Vietnamese
:   Further information: [Vietnamese people in Poland](/wiki/Vietnamese_people_in_Poland "Vietnamese people in Poland")

    About 30,000 Vietnamese lived in Poland in 2015, primarily in large cities.[[34]](#cite_note-34) They publish a number of pro- and anti-Communist newspapers. The first immigrants were Vietnamese students at Polish universities after World War II. Their numbers increased slightly during the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War"), when agreements between the Vietnamese and Polish governments allowed Vietnamese guest workers to receive industrial training in Poland. A large number of Vietnamese immigrants arrived after 1989.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

Others
:   Groups of [Americans](/wiki/United_States "United States") (1,541 in 2002, 992 of whom had Polish citizenship), [Britons](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), [Turks](/wiki/Turkish_people "Turkish people") (232, including 74 Polish citizens), [Hungarians](/wiki/Hungarians "Hungarians") (579, including 228 Polish citizens), [Italians](/wiki/Italians "Italians") (1,367, including 835 Polish citizens), [Serbs](/wiki/Serbs "Serbs"), [Croats](/wiki/Croats "Croats"), [Bulgarians](/wiki/Bulgarians "Bulgarians") (1,112, including 404 Polish citizens), [Romanians](/wiki/Romanians "Romanians"), [Georgians](/wiki/Georgian_people "Georgian people"), [Palestinians](/wiki/Palestinians "Palestinians") (229, including 146 Polish citizens) and other [Arabs](/wiki/Arabs "Arabs"), [Kurds](/wiki/Kurds "Kurds"), [Scandinavians](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia"), and [Flemings](/wiki/Flemings "Flemings") (23, including 10 Polish citizens) live in Poland.
